(Māori: [[ˈmaːɔɾi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Rar-M%C4%81ori.ogg "File:Rar-Māori.ogg"); [endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym"): ***te reo Māori*** [[tɛ ɾɛɔ ˈmaːɔɾi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori"), 'the Māori language', also shortened to ***te reo***) is an [Eastern Polynesian language](/wiki/Eastern_Polynesian_languages "Eastern Polynesian languages") and the language of the [Māori people](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"), the indigenous population of mainland [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"). The southernmost member of the [Austronesian language](/wiki/Austronesian_language "Austronesian language") family, it is related to [Cook Islands Māori](/wiki/Cook_Islands_M%C4%81ori "Cook Islands Māori"), [Tuamotuan](/wiki/Tuamotuan_language "Tuamotuan language"), and [Tahitian](/wiki/Tahitian_language "Tahitian language"). The [Māori Language Act 1987](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Language_Act_1987 "Māori Language Act 1987") gave the language recognition as one of New Zealand's [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language"). There are [regional dialects](/wiki/Regional_dialect "Regional dialect") of the Māori language.[[4]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Māori_language-4)

**Māori** (Māori: [[ˈmaːɔɾi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Rar-M%C4%81ori.ogg "File:Rar-Māori.ogg"); [endonym](/wiki/Endonym "Endonym"): ***te reo Māori*** [[tɛ ɾɛɔ ˈmaːɔɾi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori"), 'the Māori language', also shortened to ***te reo***) is an [Eastern Polynesian language](/wiki/Eastern_Polynesian_languages "Eastern Polynesian languages") and the language of the [Māori people](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_people "Māori people"), the indigenous population of mainland [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"). The southernmost member of the [Austronesian language](/wiki/Austronesian_language "Austronesian language") family, it is related to [Cook Islands Māori](/wiki/Cook_Islands_M%C4%81ori "Cook Islands Māori"), [Tuamotuan](/wiki/Tuamotuan_language "Tuamotuan language"), and [Tahitian](/wiki/Tahitian_language "Tahitian language"). The [Māori Language Act 1987](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Language_Act_1987 "Māori Language Act 1987") gave the language recognition as one of New Zealand's [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language"). There are [regional dialects](/wiki/Regional_dialect "Regional dialect") of the Māori language.[[4]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Māori_language-4)
Prior to contact with Europeans, Māori lacked a written language or script.[[a]](#cite_note-6) Written Māori now uses the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script"), which was adopted and the spelling standardised by Northern Māori in collaboration with English Protestant clergy in the 19th century.

In the second half of the 19th century, European children in rural areas spoke Māori with Māori children. It was common for prominent parents of these children, such as government officials, to use Māori in the community.[[4]](#cite_note-History_of_the_Māori_language-4)[[6]](#cite_note-:7-7) Māori declined due to the increase of the European population and government-imposed educational policies; by the early 20th century its use was banned in school playgrounds and classrooms across the country. The number of speakers fell sharply after 1945,[[7]](#cite_note-:22-8) but a [Māori language revival](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language_revival "Māori language revival") movement began in the late 20th century and slowed the decline. The [Māori protest movement](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_protest_movement "Māori protest movement") and the [Māori renaissance](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_renaissance "Māori renaissance") of the 1970s caused greater social awareness of and support for the language.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

The [2018 New Zealand census](/wiki/2018_New_Zealand_census "2018 New Zealand census") reported that about 190,000 people, or 4% of the population, could hold an everyday conversation in Māori. As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit), 55% of Māori adults reported some knowledge of the language; of these, 64% use Māori at home and around 50,000 people can speak the language "well".[[9]](#cite_note-tereoMaori2-10) As of 2023, around 7% of New Zealand primary and secondary school students are taught fully or partially in Māori, and another 24% learn Māori as an [additional language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language").[[10]](#cite_note-:11-11)

In [Māori culture](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_culture "Māori culture"), the language is considered to be among the greatest of all *[taonga](/wiki/Taonga "Taonga")*, or cultural treasures.[[11]](#cite_note-12)[[12]](#cite_note-13) Māori is known for its metaphorical poetry and prose,[[13]](#cite_note-:8-14)[[14]](#cite_note-:9-15) often in the form of *[karakia](/wiki/Karakia "Karakia")*, *whaikōrero*, *[whakapapa](/wiki/Whakapapa "Whakapapa")* and *[karanga](/wiki/Karanga_(M%C4%81ori_culture) "Karanga (Māori culture)")*, and in performing arts such as *[mōteatea](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_music#Mōteatea "Māori music")*, *[waiata](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_music "Māori music")*, and *[haka](/wiki/Haka "Haka")*.[[15]](#cite_note-:10-16)

## Name

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Name")]

The English word *[Maori](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Maori#English "wikt:Maori")* is a borrowing from the Māori language, where it is spelled *[Māori](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/M%C4%81ori#Maori "wikt:Māori")*. In New Zealand, the Māori language is often referred to as *te reo* [[tɛ ˈɾɛ.ɔ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori") ("the language"), short for *te reo Māori* ("the Māori language").[[16]](#cite_note-Higgins-17)

The Māori-language spelling with a macron ⟨*Māori*⟩ has become common in [New Zealand English](/wiki/New_Zealand_English "New Zealand English") in recent years, particularly in Māori-specific cultural contexts,[[16]](#cite_note-Higgins-17)[[17]](#cite_note-Language_Act-18) although the traditional macron-less English spelling is still sometimes seen in general media and government use.[[18]](#cite_note-19)

The pronunciation in Māori and current standard New Zealand English is /ˈma:ɔri/,[[19]](#cite_note-NZOD-20) with the 'r' usually a [flap](/wiki/Flap_consonant "Flap consonant").[[20]](#cite_note-Shields126-21) A more anglicised pronunciation is [/ˈmaʊriː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English").[[19]](#cite_note-NZOD-20)

New Zealand has two *[de jure](/wiki/De_jure "De jure")* official languages: Māori and [New Zealand Sign Language](/wiki/New_Zealand_Sign_Language "New Zealand Sign Language"),[[21]](#cite_note-22) whereas English acts as a *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* official language.[[22]](#cite_note-royal-society-nz-23)[[23]](#cite_note-stuff-nz-24) *Te reo Māori* gained its official status with the passing of the [Māori Language Act 1987](/wiki/Maori_Language_Act_1987 "Maori Language Act 1987").[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Most government departments and agencies have bilingual names—for example, the [Department of Internal Affairs](/wiki/Department_of_Internal_Affairs_(New_Zealand) "Department of Internal Affairs (New Zealand)") is alternatively *Te Tari Taiwhenua*—and places such as local government offices and public libraries display bilingual signs and use bilingual stationery; some government services now even use the Māori version solely as the official name.[[25]](#cite_note-26) Personal dealings with government agencies may be conducted in Māori, but in practice, this almost always requires [interpreters](/wiki/Language_interpretation "Language interpretation"), restricting its everyday use to the limited geographical areas of high Māori fluency, and to more formal occasions, such as during [public consultation](/wiki/Public_consultation "Public consultation"). An interpreter is on hand at sessions of the [New Zealand Parliament](/wiki/New_Zealand_Parliament "New Zealand Parliament") for instances when a member wishes to speak in Māori.[[17]](#cite_note-Language_Act-18)[[26]](#cite_note-27) Māori may be spoken in judicial proceedings, but any party wishing to do so must notify the court in advance to ensure an interpreter is available. Failure to notify in advance does not preclude the party speaking in Māori, but the court must be adjourned until an interpreter is available and the party may be held liable for the costs of the delay.[[27]](#cite_note-28)

A 1994 ruling by the [Judicial Committee of the Privy Council](/wiki/Judicial_Committee_of_the_Privy_Council "Judicial Committee of the Privy Council") (then New Zealand's highest court) held the Government responsible under the [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") (1840) for the preservation of the language.[[28]](#cite_note-29) Accordingly, since March 2004, the state has funded [Māori Television](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Television "Māori Television"), broadcast partly in Māori. On 28 March 2008, Māori Television launched its second channel, [Te Reo](/wiki/Te_Reo_(TV_channel) "Te Reo (TV channel)"), broadcast entirely in the Māori language, with no advertising or subtitles. The first Māori TV channel, [Aotearoa Television Network](/wiki/Aotearoa_Television_Network "Aotearoa Television Network") (ATN) was available to viewers in the Auckland region from 1996 but lasted for only one year.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

In 2008, [Land Information New Zealand](/wiki/Land_Information_New_Zealand "Land Information New Zealand") published the first list of official place names with [macrons](/wiki/Macron_(diacritic) "Macron (diacritic)"). Previous place name lists were derived from computer systems (usually mapping and [geographic information systems](/wiki/Geographic_information_system "Geographic information system")) that could not handle macrons.[[30]](#cite_note-31)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Political dimensions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Political dimensions")]

The official status of Māori, and especially its use in official names and titles, is a political issue in New Zealand. In 2022 a 70,000-strong petition from [Te Pāti Māori](/wiki/Te_P%C4%81ti_M%C4%81ori "Te Pāti Māori") went to Parliament calling for New Zealand to be officially renamed [Aotearoa](/wiki/Aotearoa "Aotearoa"), and was accepted for debate by the Māori Affairs select committee.[[31]](#cite_note-teAoNews-32) During [New Zealand First](/wiki/New_Zealand_First "New Zealand First")'s successful campaign to return to Parliament in 2023, party leader [Winston Peters](/wiki/Winston_Peters "Winston Peters") ridiculed the proposal as "ideological mumbo jumbo"[[32]](#cite_note-1News_Englishofficial-33) and criticised the use of the name in government reports.[[33]](#cite_note-Herald_NZFirst-34) Peters promised his party would remove Māori names from government departments,[[34]](#cite_note-1News_removeMāorinames-35) saying "[Te Whatu Ora](/wiki/Te_Whatu_Ora "Te Whatu Ora"), excuse me, I don't want to speak the Māori language when I go to hospital."[[35]](#cite_note-Herald_notindigenous-36) As part of its coalition agreement with New Zealand First, the [National](/wiki/New_Zealand_National_Party "New Zealand National Party")-led government agreed to ensure all public service departments had their primary name in English except for those specifically related to Māori.[[36]](#cite_note-1news_WakaKotahi-37)[[37]](#cite_note-RNZ-38)

In 2025, [Radio New Zealand](/wiki/Radio_New_Zealand "Radio New Zealand") reported that education minister [Erica Stanford](/wiki/Erica_Stanford "Erica Stanford") had decided the previous year to exclude most Māori words from the Education Ministry's "Ready to Read Phonics Plus" series. Stanford said that the decision only affected 12 books within the series and that 27 books with Māori words would be reprinted.[[38]](#cite_note-39) The decision was described as "over-reach" by the New Zealand Principals' Federation[[39]](#cite_note-40) and sharply criticised as an "act of racism" by writers and publishers.[[40]](#cite_note-41)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: History")]

### Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Origins")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/First_Lessons_in_the_Maori_Language%2C_title_page_1862.jpeg/250px-First_Lessons_in_the_Maori_Language%2C_title_page_1862.jpeg)](/wiki/File:First_Lessons_in_the_Maori_Language,_title_page_1862.jpeg)

"First Lessons in the Maori Language", 1862,  
by [W. L. Williams](/wiki/Leonard_Williams_(bishop) "Leonard Williams (bishop)"), third [Bishop of Waiapu](/wiki/Anglican_Diocese_of_Waiapu "Anglican Diocese of Waiapu")

According to [legend](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_mythology "Māori mythology"), Māori came to New Zealand from [Hawaiki](/wiki/Hawaiki "Hawaiki"). Current anthropological thinking places their origin in eastern [Polynesia](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia"), mostly likely from the [Southern Cook](/wiki/Southern_Cook_Islands "Southern Cook Islands") or [Society Islands](/wiki/Society_Islands "Society Islands") region (see [Māori history § Origins from Polynesia](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_history#Origins_from_Polynesia "Māori history")), and says that they arrived by deliberate [voyages in seagoing canoes](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_migration_canoes "Māori migration canoes"),[[41]](#cite_note-42) possibly double-hulled, and probably sail-rigged. These settlers probably arrived by AD 1350 at the latest.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Māori evolved in isolation from other sister [Polynesian languages](/wiki/Polynesian_languages "Polynesian languages") adapting to New Zealand's unique [geographical](/wiki/Geography_of_New_Zealand#Physical_geography "Geography of New Zealand") features and [temperate climate](/wiki/Climate_of_New_Zealand "Climate of New Zealand") (including presence of snow and loss of tropical floral inventory).[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs1994b21–29-44) Six dialectal variations emerged among [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") due to further internal geographical separation.[[44]](#cite_note-Te_Reo_English-45) The language had no written form, but historian Sarah J. K. Gallagher has argued that [tā moko](/wiki/T%C4%81_moko "Tā moko"), the indigenous art of tattooing, is arguably "a pre-European textual culture in New Zealand... as the Moko can be read, it can be accepted as a form of communication".[[45]](#cite_note-46) The idea that tā moko is a written language of sorts has been discussed before.[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48)

Since its origin, the Māori language has been rich in metaphorical poetry and prose.[[13]](#cite_note-:8-14)[[14]](#cite_note-:9-15) Forms of this include [karakia](/wiki/Karakia "Karakia"), [whaikōrero](/wiki/Whakapapa "Whakapapa"), [whakapapa](/wiki/Whakapapa "Whakapapa") and [karanga](/wiki/Karanga_(M%C4%81ori_culture) "Karanga (Māori culture)"), and in [performing arts](/wiki/Performing_arts "Performing arts") such as [mōteatea](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_music#Mōteatea "Māori music"), [waiata](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_music "Māori music") and [haka](/wiki/Haka "Haka").[[15]](#cite_note-:10-16) Karakia are Māori [incantations](/wiki/Incantation "Incantation") used to invoke spiritual guidance and protection, and are used before eating or gathering, to increase spiritual goodwill and to declare things officially open.[[48]](#cite_note-Otago-49) Whaikōrero is the term given to traditional oratory given on [marae](/wiki/Marae "Marae"), and whakapapa is the story of one's ancestry. According to historian [Atholl Anderson](/wiki/Atholl_Anderson "Atholl Anderson"), whakapapa used "mnemonic devices, repetitive patterns [and] rhyme" to leave a lasting impression. "Casting knowledge in formulaic or other standarised story forms... helped to fix the information in the minds of speakers and listeners".[[49]](#cite_note-50)

### European contact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: European contact")]

Missionaries learned to speak Māori, and introduced the Latin alphabet to Māori. The [Church Mission Society](/wiki/Church_Mission_Society "Church Mission Society") (CMS), including [Thomas Kendall](/wiki/Thomas_Kendall "Thomas Kendall"); Māori, including [Tītore](/wiki/T%C4%ABtore "Tītore") and [Hongi Hika](/wiki/Hongi_Hika "Hongi Hika"); and [Cambridge University](/wiki/Cambridge_University "Cambridge University")'s [Samuel Lee](/wiki/Samuel_Lee_(linguist) "Samuel Lee (linguist)"), developed the written form of the language between 1817 and 1830. In 1833, while living in the [Paihia](/wiki/Paihia "Paihia") mission-house of [Anglican](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") priest and the now head of the [New Zealand CMS](/wiki/New_Zealand_Church_Missionary_Society "New Zealand Church Missionary Society") mission, Rev [Henry Williams](/wiki/Henry_Williams_(missionary) "Henry Williams (missionary)"), missioner [William Colenso](/wiki/William_Colenso "William Colenso") published Māori translations including parts of books of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), the first books printed in New Zealand. Colenso's 1837 Māori New Testament was the first [indigenous-language translation of the Bible](/wiki/Bible_translations_into_Oceanic_languages "Bible translations into Oceanic languages") published in the southern hemisphere. Demand for the Māori New Testament, and for the Prayer Book that followed, grew exponentially, as did Christian Māori [leadership](/wiki/Leadership "Leadership") and public Christian services, with 33,000 Māori soon attending regularly. Literacy and understanding the Bible increased *[mana](/wiki/Mana_(Oceanian_mythology) "Mana (Oceanian mythology)")* and social and economic benefits.[[50]](#cite_note-B&T_I-51) Worship took place in Māori; it functioned as the language of Māori homes; Māori politicians conducted political meetings in Māori; and some literature appeared in Māori, along with many newspapers.[[51]](#cite_note-history-52) Before 1880, some Māori parliamentarians suffered disadvantages because parliamentary proceedings took place in English.[[52]](#cite_note-53) However, by 1900, all Māori members of parliament, such as [Āpirana Ngata](/wiki/%C4%80pirana_Ngata "Āpirana Ngata"), were university graduates who spoke fluent English.[[44]](#cite_note-Te_Reo_English-45)

### Suppression and decline

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Suppression and decline")]

Efforts to culturally assimilate Māori into European society began in the 19th century. This included policies to educate Māori based on the English school system, with steps taken that increasingly promoted English as the sole language of use in schools.[[53]](#cite_note-54) These adversely affected the use of the Maori language by Maori children. The Education Ordinance Act 1847 set English as the predominant language of schools.[[54]](#cite_note-rnz-petition-55) This was followed in 1880 by the Native Schools Code, which placed further restrictions on the use of Māori, establishing the expectation that teachers would have some knowledge of the language solely for the purpose of teaching English to younger pupils. Further restrictions on Māori followed, to the point that in the early twentieth century, children were forbidden to speak it in the classroom or playground, under penalty of [corporal punishment](/wiki/Corporal_punishment "Corporal punishment").[[55]](#cite_note-ka'ai-mahuta-56) In recent years, prominent Māori have spoken with sadness about their experiences or experiences of their family members being caned, strapped or beaten in school.[[56]](#cite_note-57)[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)

In many cases these policies were accepted or even encouraged by parents who wanted their children to succeed in the Pākehā world around them;[[59]](#cite_note-Calman2-60) however, it remained government policy to educate Māori in manual trades rather than academic professions until the mid-twentieth century.[[60]](#cite_note-auditor-general-61) Proposals in 1930 to introduce Māori to the curriculum were blocked on the grounds that the purpose of education was to "lead the Māori lad to be a good farmer and the Māori girl to be a good farmer's wife".[[60]](#cite_note-auditor-general-61)

Most Māori people continued to speak Māori as their first language until [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). The number of speakers of Māori began to decline rapidly with the migration of Māori to urban areas after the war (the [urban Māori](/wiki/Urban_M%C4%81ori "Urban Māori")).[[51]](#cite_note-history-52) By the 1980s, fewer than 20 per cent of Māori spoke the language well enough to be classed as native speakers. Even many of those people no longer spoke Māori in their homes. As a result, many Māori children failed to learn their ancestral language, and generations of non-Māori-speaking Māori emerged.[[61]](#cite_note-62)

In 1984, [Naida Glavish](/wiki/Naida_Glavish "Naida Glavish"), a [tolls operator](/wiki/Switchboard_operator "Switchboard operator"), was demoted for using the Māori greeting "*[kia ora](/wiki/Kia_ora "Kia ora")*" with customers. The "[Kia Ora Incident](/wiki/Kia_Ora_Incident "Kia Ora Incident")" was the subject of public and political scrutiny before having her job reinstated by Prime Minister [Robert Muldoon](/wiki/Robert_Muldoon "Robert Muldoon"), and became a major symbol of long-standing [linguicism](/wiki/Linguicism "Linguicism") in New Zealand.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

### Revitalisation efforts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Revitalisation efforts")]

Main article: [Māori language revival](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language_revival "Māori language revival")

By the 1950s some Māori leaders had begun to recognise the dangers of the loss of *te reo Māori*.[[66]](#cite_note-:6-67) By the 1970s there were many strategies used to save the language.[[66]](#cite_note-:6-67) This included Māori-language revitalisation programs such as the [Kōhanga Reo](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language_revival#Kōhanga_Reo "Māori language revival") movement, which from 1982 immersed infants in Māori from infancy to school age.[[67]](#cite_note-:2-68) There followed in 1985 the founding of the first [Kura Kaupapa Māori](/wiki/Kura_Kaupapa_M%C4%81ori "Kura Kaupapa Māori") (Years 1 to 8 Māori-medium education programme) and later the first Wharekura (Years 9 to 13 Māori-medium education programme). In 2011 it was reported that although "there was a true revival of te reo in the 1980s and early to mid-1990s ... spurred on by the realisation of how few speakers were left, and by the relative abundance of older fluent speakers in both urban neighbourhoods and rural communities", the language has continued to decline."[[67]](#cite_note-:2-68) The decline is believed "to have several underlying causes".[[68]](#cite_note-Waitangi_Tribunal_2011,_p._440-69) These include:

* the ongoing loss of older native speakers who have spearheaded the [Māori-language revival](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language_revival "Māori language revival") movement
* complacency brought about by the very existence of the institutions which drove the revival
* concerns about quality, with the supply of good teachers never matching demand (even while that demand has been shrinking)
* excessive regulation and centralised control, which has alienated some of those involved in the movement
* an ongoing lack of educational resources needed to teach the full curriculum in *te reo Māori*[[68]](#cite_note-Waitangi_Tribunal_2011,_p._440-69)
* natural [language attrition](/wiki/Language_attrition "Language attrition") caused by the overwhelming increase of spoken English.

Based on the principles of partnership, Māori-speaking government, general revitalisation and dialectal protective policy, and adequate resourcing, the [Waitangi Tribunal](/wiki/Waitangi_Tribunal "Waitangi Tribunal") has recommended "four fundamental changes":[[69]](#cite_note-70)

1. [Te Taura Whiri](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Language_Commission "Māori Language Commission") (the Māori Language Commission) should become the lead Māori language sector agency. This will address the problems caused by the lack of ownership and leadership identified by the Office of the Auditor-General.[[70]](#cite_note-71)
2. Te Taura Whiri should function as a Crown–Māori partnership through the equal appointment of Crown and Māori appointees to its board. This reflects [the Tribunal's] concern that te reo revival will not work if responsibility for setting the direction is not shared with Māori.
3. Te Taura Whiri will also need increased powers. This will ensure that public bodies are compelled to contribute to *te reo*'s revival and that key agencies are held properly accountable for the strategies they adopt. For instance, targets for the training of te reo teachers must be met, education curricula involving te reo must be approved, and public bodies in districts with a sufficient number and/or proportion of *te reo* speakers and schools with a certain proportion of Māori students must submit Māori language plans for approval.
4. These regional public bodies and schools must also consult [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") (Māori tribes or tribal confederations) in the preparation of their plans. In this way, iwi will come to have a central role in the revitalisation of *te reo* in their own areas. This should encourage efforts to promote the language at the grassroots.[[71]](#cite_note-72)

The changes set forth by the Tribunal are merely recommendations; they are not binding upon government.[[72]](#cite_note-:1-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Bastion_Point_Maori_Land_Rights_copy.jpg/250px-Bastion_Point_Maori_Land_Rights_copy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bastion_Point_Maori_Land_Rights_copy.jpg)

[Bastion Point](/wiki/Bastion_Point "Bastion Point") land rights activists with Māori-language signs

There is, however, evidence that the revitalisation efforts are taking hold, as can be seen in the teaching of *te reo* in the school curriculum, the use of Māori as an instructional language, and the supportive ideologies surrounding these efforts.[[73]](#cite_note-:0-74) In 2014, a survey of students ranging in age from 18 to 24 was conducted; the students were of mixed ethnic backgrounds, ranging from *[Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā")* to Māori who lived in New Zealand. This survey showed a 62% response saying that *te reo Māori* was at risk.[[73]](#cite_note-:0-74) Albury argues that these results come from the language either not being used enough in common discourse, or from the fact that the number of speakers was inadequate for future language development.[[73]](#cite_note-:0-74)

The policies for language revitalisation have been changing in attempts to improve Māori language use and have been working with suggestions from the Waitangi Tribunal on the best ways to implement the revitalisation. The Waitangi Tribunal in 2011 identified a suggestion for language revitalisation that would shift indigenous policies from the central government to the preferences and ideologies of the Māori people.[[72]](#cite_note-:1-73) This change recognises the issue of Māori revitalisation as one of indigenous self-determination, instead of the job of the government to identify what would be best for the language and Māori people of New Zealand.[[74]](#cite_note-:4-75)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Broadwood_Area_School2.JPG/250px-Broadwood_Area_School2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Broadwood_Area_School2.JPG)

Bilingual sign in [Broadwood, Northland](/wiki/Broadwood,_New_Zealand "Broadwood, New Zealand")

### Revival since 2015

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Revival since 2015")]

Beginning in about 2015, the Māori language underwent a revival as it became increasingly popular, as a common national heritage and shared cultural identity, even among New Zealanders without Māori roots. Surveys from 2018 indicated that "the Māori language currently enjoys a high status in Māori society and also positive acceptance by the majority of non-Māori New Zealanders".[[75]](#cite_note-The_Guardian_28_July_2018-76)[[76]](#cite_note-:5-77)

As the status and prestige of the language rose, so did the demand for language classes. Businesses, including Google, Microsoft, [Vodafone NZ](/wiki/Vodafone_New_Zealand "Vodafone New Zealand") and [Fletcher Building](/wiki/Fletcher_Building "Fletcher Building"), were quick to adopt the trend as it became apparent that using *te reo* made customers think of a company as "committed to New Zealand". The language became increasingly heard in the media and in politics. Prime Minister [Jacinda Ardern](/wiki/Jacinda_Ardern "Jacinda Ardern")—who gave her daughter a Māori middle name, and said she would learn both Māori and English—made headlines when she toasted Commonwealth leaders in 2018 with a Māori proverb, and the success of Māori musical groups such as [Alien Weaponry](/wiki/Alien_Weaponry "Alien Weaponry") and [Maimoa](/wiki/Maimoa "Maimoa") further increased the language's presence in social media.[[75]](#cite_note-The_Guardian_28_July_2018-76)[[76]](#cite_note-:5-77)[[77]](#cite_note-78) Since 2017, a selected number of [Disney](/wiki/The_Walt_Disney_Company "The Walt Disney Company") movies have received a [dubbing](/wiki/Dubbing "Dubbing") in Māori,[[78]](#cite_note-79)[[79]](#cite_note-80) the first having been *[Moana](/wiki/Moana_(2016_film) "Moana (2016 film)")*.[[80]](#cite_note-81)

In August 2017, [Rotorua](/wiki/Rotorua "Rotorua") became the first city in New Zealand to declare itself as [bilingual](/wiki/Bilingual "Bilingual") in the Māori and English languages, meaning that both languages would be promoted. In 2019, the New Zealand government launched the *Maihi Karauna* Māori language revitalisation strategy with a goal of 1 million people speaking *te reo Māori* by 2040.[[81]](#cite_note-82)[[82]](#cite_note-83) Also in 2019, Kotahi Rau Pukapuka Trust and [Auckland University Press](/wiki/Auckland_University_Press "Auckland University Press") began work on publishing a sizeable library of local and international literature in the language, including the [Harry Potter](/wiki/Harry_Potter "Harry Potter") books.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

Some New Zealanders have pushed against the revival, debating the replacement of English-language place names with original Māori names, criticising a police car having Māori language and graphics, and complaining about *te reo Māori* being used by broadcasters.[[76]](#cite_note-:5-77) In March 2021, the [Broadcasting Standards Authority](/wiki/Broadcasting_Standards_Authority "Broadcasting Standards Authority") (BSA) said it would no longer entertain complaints regarding the use of the Māori language in broadcasts. This followed a fivefold increase in complaints to the BSA. The use of Māori in itself does not breach any broadcasting standards.[[84]](#cite_note-85)

Comparative linguists classify Māori as a [Polynesian language](/wiki/Polynesian_languages "Polynesian languages"), specifically as an [Eastern Polynesian language](/wiki/Eastern_Polynesian_languages "Eastern Polynesian languages") belonging to the [Tahitic](/wiki/Tahitic_languages "Tahitic languages") subgroup, which includes [Cook Islands Māori](/wiki/Cook_Islands_M%C4%81ori "Cook Islands Māori"), spoken in the southern [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands"), and [Tahitian](/wiki/Tahitian_language "Tahitian language"), spoken in [Tahiti](/wiki/Tahiti "Tahiti") and the [Society Islands](/wiki/Society_Islands "Society Islands"). Other major Eastern Polynesian languages include [Hawaiian](/wiki/Hawaiian_language "Hawaiian language"), [Marquesan](/wiki/Marquesan_language "Marquesan language") (languages in the [Marquesic](/wiki/Marquesic_languages "Marquesic languages") subgroup), and the [Rapa Nui language](/wiki/Rapa_Nui_language "Rapa Nui language") of [Easter Island](/wiki/Easter_Island "Easter Island").[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199496–105-86)[[86]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark1994123–135-87)[[87]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow1994106–122-88)

While the preceding are all distinct languages, they remain similar enough that [Tupaia](/wiki/Tupaia_(navigator) "Tupaia (navigator)"), a Tahitian travelling with Captain [James Cook](/wiki/James_Cook "James Cook") in 1769–1770, communicated effectively with Māori.[[88]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBanks17719_October_1769:_"we_again_advanc'd_to_the_river_side_with_Tupia,_who_now_found_that_the_language_of_the_people_was_so_like_his_own_that_he_could_tolerably_well_understand_them_and_they_him."-89) Hawaiian newspaper *[Ka Nupepa Kuokoa](/wiki/Ka_Nupepa_Kuokoa "Ka Nupepa Kuokoa")* in 1911 covering [Ernest Kaʻai](/wiki/Ernest_Ka%CA%BBai "Ernest Kaʻai") and his Royal Hawaiians' band tour of New Zealand reported that Kaʻai himself wrote to them about the band able to communicate with Māori while visiting their rural [maraes](/wiki/Marae "Marae").[[89]](#cite_note-90) Māori actors, travelling to Easter Island for production of the film *[Rapa-Nui](/wiki/Rapa-Nui_(film) "Rapa-Nui (film)")* noticed a marked similarity between the native tongues, as did arts curator Reuben Friend, who noted that it took only a short time to pick up any different vocabulary and the different nuances to recognisable words.[[90]](#cite_note-91) Speakers of modern Māori generally report that they find the languages of the [Cook Islands](/wiki/Cook_Islands "Cook Islands"), including Rarotongan, the easiest among the other Polynesian languages to understand and converse in.

## Geographic distribution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Geographic distribution")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/TeReoMaori2013.png/250px-TeReoMaori2013.png)](/wiki/File:TeReoMaori2013.png)

Nearly all speakers are ethnic Māori residents of New Zealand. Estimates of the number of speakers vary: the 1996 census reported 160,000,[[91]](#cite_note-quickstats-92) while a 1995 national survey reported about 10,000 "very fluent" adult speakers.[[92]](#cite_note-93)
As reported in the 2013 national census, only 21.3% of self-identified Māori had a conversational knowledge of the language, and only around 6.5% of those speakers, 1.4% of the total Māori population, spoke the Māori language only. This percentage has been in decline in recent years, from around a quarter of the population[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] to 21%. In the same census, Māori speakers formed 3.7% of the total population.[[93]](#cite_note-Māori_language_speakers-94)

The level of competence of self-professed Māori speakers varies from minimal to total. Statistics have not been gathered for the prevalence of different levels of competence. Only a minority of self-professed speakers use Māori as their main language at home.[[94]](#cite_note-:3-95) The rest use only a few words or phrases ([passive](/wiki/Passive_speakers_(language) "Passive speakers (language)") bilingualism).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Māori still[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit) is a community language in some predominantly Māori settlements in the [Northland](/wiki/Northland_Region "Northland Region"), [Urewera](/wiki/Te_Urewera "Te Urewera") and [East Cape](/wiki/East_Cape "East Cape") areas. *[Kōhanga reo](/wiki/K%C5%8Dhanga_reo "Kōhanga reo")* Māori-immersion kindergartens throughout New Zealand exclusively use Māori.[[94]](#cite_note-:3-95)

Urbanisation after the Second World War led to widespread language shift from Māori predominance (with Māori the primary language of the rural *[whānau](/wiki/Wh%C4%81nau "Whānau")*) to English predominance (English serving as the primary language in the *[Pākehā](/wiki/P%C4%81keh%C4%81 "Pākehā")* cities). Therefore, Māori speakers almost always communicate bilingually, with [New Zealand English](/wiki/New_Zealand_English "New Zealand English") as either their first or second language. Only around 9,000 people exclusively speak in Māori.[[74]](#cite_note-:4-75)

In the 2023 school year, around 7.2% of primary and secondary school students in New Zealand were taught fully or partially in Māori. An additional 24.4% were formally taught Māori as an additional language, and 37.1% were taught Māori informally. However, very few students pass through the New Zealand education system without any Māori language education. For example, only 2.1% of students in Year 1 (aged 5) did not receive any Māori language education in 2023.[[10]](#cite_note-:11-11)

The use of the Māori language in the Māori diaspora is far lower than in New Zealand itself. Census data from Australia show it as the home language of 11,747, just 8.2% of the total Australian Māori population in 2016.[[95]](#cite_note-96)

## Orthography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Orthography")]

See also: [Māori Braille](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Braille "Māori Braille")

The modern Māori [alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet "Alphabet") has 15 letters, two of which are [digraphs](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") (character pairs). The five vowels have both short and long forms, with the long forms denoted by macrons marked above them.

Māori letters

| Consonants | Vowels | |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Short | Long |
| * [H](/wiki/H "H") h * [K](/wiki/K "K") k * [M](/wiki/M "M") m * [N](/wiki/N "N") n * [P](/wiki/P "P") p * [R](/wiki/R "R") r * [T](/wiki/T "T") t * [W](/wiki/W "W") w * Ng ng * Wh wh | * [A](/wiki/A "A") a * [E](/wiki/E "E") e * [I](/wiki/I "I") i * [O](/wiki/O "O") o * [U](/wiki/U "U") u | * [Ā](/wiki/%C4%80 "Ā") ā * [Ē](/wiki/%C4%92 "Ē") ē * [Ī](/wiki/%C4%AA "Ī") ī * [Ō](/wiki/%C5%8C_(disambiguation) "Ō (disambiguation)") ō * [Ū](/wiki/%C5%AA "Ū") ū |

The order of the alphabet is as follows: A, E, H, I, K, M, N, O, P, R, T, U, W, Ng, Wh.

This standard orthography may be tweaked to represent certain dialects of Māori:

* An underlined "ḵ" sometimes appears when writing the Southern dialect, to indicate that the /k/ in question corresponds to the *ng* of the standard language.
* Both [L](/wiki/L "L") and [G](/wiki/G "G") are also encountered in the [Southern dialect](#South_Island_dialects), though not in standard Māori.
* Various methods are used to indicate glottal stops when writing the [Whanganui](/wiki/Whanganui "Whanganui") dialect.

### History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: History")]

There was originally no native writing system for Māori. It has been suggested that the [petroglyphs](/wiki/Petroglyphs "Petroglyphs") once used by the Māori developed into a script similar to the [Rongorongo](/wiki/Rongorongo "Rongorongo") of Easter Island.[[96]](#cite_note-97) However, there is no evidence that these petroglyphs ever evolved into a true system of writing. Some distinctive markings among the *kōwhaiwhai* (rafter paintings) of meeting houses were used as mnemonics in reciting *whakapapa* (genealogy) but again, there was no systematic relation between marks and meanings.

Attempts to write Māori words using the [Latin script](/wiki/Latin_script "Latin script") began with Captain James Cook and other early explorers, with varying degrees of success. Consonants seem to have caused the most difficulty, but medial and final vowels are often missing in early sources. [Anne Salmond](/wiki/Anne_Salmond_(historian) "Anne Salmond (historian)")[[97]](#cite_note-Salmond_1997-98) records *aghee* for aki (in the year 1773, from the North Island East Coast, p. 98), *Toogee* and *E tanga roak* for Tuki and Tangaroa (1793, Northland, p. 216), *Kokramea*, *Kakramea* for Kakaramea (1801, Hauraki, p. 261), *toges* for tokis, *Wannugu* for Uenuku and *gumera* for kumara (1801, Hauraki, pp. 261, 266 and 269), *Weygate* for Waikato (1801, Hauraki, p. 277), *Bunga Bunga* for pungapunga, *tubua* for tupua and *gure* for kurī (1801, Hauraki, p. 279), as well as *Tabooha* for Te Puhi (1823, Northern Northland, p. 385).

From 1814, missionaries tried to define the sounds of the language. [Thomas Kendall](/wiki/Thomas_Kendall "Thomas Kendall") published a book in 1815 entitled *[A korao no New Zealand](/wiki/A_korao_no_New_Zealand "A korao no New Zealand")*, which in modern orthography and usage would be *He Kōrero nō Aotearoa*. Beginning in 1817, professor [Samuel Lee](/wiki/Samuel_Lee_(linguist) "Samuel Lee (linguist)") of [Cambridge University](/wiki/Cambridge_University "Cambridge University") worked with the [Ngāpuhi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81puhi "Ngāpuhi") chief [Tītore](/wiki/T%C4%ABtore "Tītore") and his junior relative Tui (also known as Tuhi or Tupaea),[[98]](#cite_note-TIT3-99) and then with chief [Hongi Hika](/wiki/Hongi_Hika "Hongi Hika")[[99]](#cite_note-100) and his junior relative [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato_(rangatira) "Waikato (rangatira)"); they established a definitive orthography based on Northern usage, published as the *First Grammar and Vocabulary of the New Zealand Language* (1820).[[98]](#cite_note-TIT3-99) The missionaries of the [Church Missionary Society (CMS)](/wiki/New_Zealand_Church_Missionary_Society "New Zealand Church Missionary Society") did not have a high regard for this book. By 1830 the CMS missionaries had revised the [orthography](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") for writing the Māori language; for example, "Kiddeekiddee" was changed to the modern spelling, "[Kerikeri](/wiki/Kerikeri "Kerikeri")".[[100]](#cite_note-TMR55-101)[*[non-primary source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")*]

The Māori embraced [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") enthusiastically, and missionaries reported in the 1820s that Māori all over the country taught each other to read and write, using sometimes quite innovative materials in the absence of paper, such as leaves and charcoal, and [flax](/wiki/Flax_in_New_Zealand "Flax in New Zealand").[[101]](#cite_note-102) Missionary [James West Stack](/wiki/James_West_Stack "James West Stack") recorded the scarcity of slates and writing materials at the [native schools](/wiki/Native_schools "Native schools") and the use sometimes of "pieces of board on which sand was sprinkled, and the letters traced upon the sand with a pointed stick".[[102]](#cite_note-103)

#### Long vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Long vowels")]

The alphabet devised at Cambridge University does not mark vowel length. The examples in the following table show that vowel length is phonemic in Māori.

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| *ata* | morning | ***ā**ta* | carefully |
| *keke* | cake | *k**ē**k**ē*** | armpit |
| *mana* | prestige | *m**ā**na* | for him/her |
| *manu* | bird | *m**ā**nu* | to float |
| *tatari* | to wait for | *t**ā**tari* | to filter or analyse |
| *tui* | to sew | *t**ūī*** | [type of bird](/wiki/T%C5%AB%C4%AB "Tūī") |
| *wahine* | woman | *w**ā**hine* | women |

Māori devised ways to mark vowel length, sporadically at first. Occasional and inconsistent vowel-length markings occur in 19th-century manuscripts and newspapers written by Māori, including macron-like [diacritics](/wiki/Diacritic "Diacritic") and doubling of letters. Māori writer [Hare Hongi (Henry Stowell)](/wiki/Henry_Matthew_Stowell "Henry Matthew Stowell") used macrons in his *Maori-English Tutor and Vade Mecum* of 1911,[[103]](#cite_note-Stowell-104) as does Sir [Āpirana Ngata](/wiki/%C4%80pirana_Ngata "Āpirana Ngata") (albeit inconsistently) in his *Maori Grammar and Conversation* (7th printing 1953). Once the Māori language was taught in universities in the 1960s, vowel-length marking was made systematic. [Bruce Biggs](/wiki/Bruce_Biggs "Bruce Biggs"), of [Ngāti Maniapoto](/wiki/Ng%C4%81ti_Maniapoto "Ngāti Maniapoto") descent and professor at the [University of Auckland](/wiki/University_of_Auckland "University of Auckland"), promoted the use of double vowels (e.g. *waahine*); this style was standard at the university until Biggs died in 2000.

Macrons (*tohutō*) are now the standard means of indicating long vowels,[[104]](#cite_note-105) after becoming the favoured option of the [Māori Language Commission](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Language_Commission "Māori Language Commission")—set up by the Māori Language Act 1987 to act as the authority for Māori spelling and orthography.[[105]](#cite_note-macrons-106)[[106]](#cite_note-107) Most news media now use macrons; [Stuff](/wiki/Stuff_(company) "Stuff (company)") websites and newspapers since 2017,[[107]](#cite_note-108) [TVNZ](/wiki/TVNZ "TVNZ")[[108]](#cite_note-109) and [NZME](/wiki/New_Zealand_Media_and_Entertainment "New Zealand Media and Entertainment") websites and newspapers since 2018.[[109]](#cite_note-110)

Technical limitations in producing macronised vowels are sometimes resolved by using a [diaeresis](/wiki/Diaeresis_(diacritic) "Diaeresis (diacritic)")[[110]](#cite_note-111) or [circumflex](/wiki/Circumflex "Circumflex")[[111]](#cite_note-112) instead of a macron (e.g., wähine or wâhine). In other cases, it is resolved by omitting the macron all together (e.g. wahine).[[112]](#cite_note-113)

Double vowels continue to be used in a few exceptional cases, including:

* The [Waikato-Tainui](/wiki/Waikato_Tainui "Waikato Tainui") iwi preference is for using doubled vowels;[[113]](#cite_note-114) hence in the [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato") region, double vowels are used by the [Hamilton City Council](/wiki/Hamilton_City_Council_(New_Zealand) "Hamilton City Council (New Zealand)"),[[114]](#cite_note-115) [Waikato District Council](/wiki/Waikato_District_Council "Waikato District Council")[[115]](#cite_note-116) and [Waikato Museum](/wiki/Waikato_Museum "Waikato Museum").
* [Inland Revenue](/wiki/Inland_Revenue_Department_(New_Zealand) "Inland Revenue Department (New Zealand)") continues to spell its Māori name *Te Tari Taake* instead of *Te Tari Tāke*, mainly to reduce the resemblance of *tāke* to the English word 'take'.[[116]](#cite_note-117)
* A considerable number of governmental and non-governmental organisations continue to use the older spelling of ⟨[roopu](/wiki/Roopu "Roopu")⟩ ('association') in their names rather than the more modern form ⟨rōpū⟩. Examples include [Te Roopu Raranga Whatu o Aotearoa](/wiki/Te_Roopu_Raranga_Whatu_o_Aotearoa "Te Roopu Raranga Whatu o Aotearoa") ('the national Māori weavers' collective') and *Te Roopu Pounamu* (a Māori-specific organisation within the [Green Party of Aotearoa New Zealand](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Aotearoa_New_Zealand "Green Party of Aotearoa New Zealand")).
* Double vowels are also used instead of macrons in long vowels resultant from [compounding](/wiki/Compound_(linguistics) "Compound (linguistics)") (e.g. [Mātaatua](/wiki/M%C4%81taatua "Mātaatua")) or [reduplication](/wiki/Reduplication "Reduplication").[[117]](#cite_note-118)

## Phonology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Phonology")]

Main article: [Māori phonology](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_phonology "Māori phonology")

Māori has five phonemically distinct vowel articulations, and ten consonant phonemes.

### Vowels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Vowels")]

|  | [Front](/wiki/Front_vowel "Front vowel") | [Central](/wiki/Central_vowel "Central vowel") | [Back](/wiki/Back_vowel "Back vowel") |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Close](/wiki/Close_vowel "Close vowel") | [i](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel"), [iː](/wiki/Close_front_unrounded_vowel "Close front unrounded vowel") | [u](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel"), [uː](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel") | |
| [Mid](/wiki/Mid_vowel "Mid vowel") | [e](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel"), [eː](/wiki/Close-mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Close-mid front unrounded vowel") |  | [o](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel"), [oː](/wiki/Close-mid_back_rounded_vowel "Close-mid back rounded vowel") |
| [Open](/wiki/Open_vowel "Open vowel") |  | [a](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel"), [aː](/wiki/Open_front_unrounded_vowel "Open front unrounded vowel") | |

Although it is commonly claimed that vowel realisations (pronunciations) in Māori show little variation, linguistic research has shown this not to be the case.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1993537-119)[[b]](#cite_note-120)

Vowel length is phonemic, but four of the five long vowels occur in only a handful of word roots, the exception being /aː/.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997536-121)[[c]](#cite_note-122) As noted above, it has recently become standard in Māori spelling to indicate a long vowel with a macron. For older speakers, long vowels tend to be more peripheral and short vowels more centralised, especially with the low vowel, which is long [aː] but short [ɐ]. For younger speakers, they are both [a]. For older speakers, /u/ is only fronted after /t/; elsewhere it is [u]. For younger speakers, it is fronted [ʉ] everywhere, as with the corresponding phoneme in [New Zealand English](/wiki/New_Zealand_English "New Zealand English"). Due to the influence of New Zealand English, the vowel [e] is raised to be near [i], so that *pī* and *kē* (or *piki* and *kete*) now largely share the very same vowel space.[[120]](#cite_note-Rev-123): 198–199

Beside [monophthongs](/wiki/Monophthong "Monophthong") Māori has many [diphthong](/wiki/Diphthong "Diphthong") vowel phonemes. Although any short vowel combinations are possible, researchers disagree on which combinations constitute diphthongs.[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow200769-124) [Formant](/wiki/Formant "Formant") frequency analysis distinguish /aĭ/, /aĕ/, /aŏ/, /aŭ/, /oŭ/ as diphthongs.[[122]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow200779-125)
As in many other Polynesian languages, diphthongs in Māori vary only slightly from sequences of adjacent vowels, except that they belong to the same syllable, and all or nearly all sequences of nonidentical vowels are possible. All sequences of nonidentical short vowels occur and are phonemically distinct.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow19961-126)[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997534-127)

### Consonants

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Consonants")]

The consonant phonemes of Māori are listed in the following table. Seven of the ten Māori [consonant letters](/wiki/Orthography "Orthography") have the same pronunciation as they do in the [International Phonetic Alphabet](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet") (IPA). For those that do not, the IPA [phonetic transcription](/wiki/Phonetic_transcription "Phonetic transcription") is included, enclosed in square brackets per IPA convention.

|  | [Labial](/wiki/Labial_consonant "Labial consonant") | [Coronal](/wiki/Coronal_consonant "Coronal consonant") | [Velar](/wiki/Velar_consonant "Velar consonant") | [Glottal](/wiki/Glottal_consonant "Glottal consonant") |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [Nasal](/wiki/Nasal_consonant "Nasal consonant") | [m](/wiki/Voiced_bilabial_nasal "Voiced bilabial nasal") | [n](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_nasal "Voiced alveolar nasal") | ng [[ŋ](/wiki/Voiced_velar_nasal "Voiced velar nasal")] |  |
| [Plosive](/wiki/Plosive_consonant "Plosive consonant") | [p](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_plosive "Voiceless bilabial plosive") | [t](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolar_plosive "Voiceless alveolar plosive") | [k](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_plosive "Voiceless velar plosive") |  |
| [Continuant](/wiki/Continuant "Continuant") | wh [[f](/wiki/Voiceless_labiodental_fricative "Voiceless labiodental fricative"), [ɸ](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_fricative "Voiceless bilabial fricative")] | r [[ɾ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_tap_or_flap "Voiced alveolar tap or flap")] | [w](/wiki/Voiced_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Voiced labial–velar approximant") | [h](/wiki/Voiceless_glottal_fricative "Voiceless glottal fricative") |

The pronunciation of ⟨wh⟩ is extremely variable,[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997On_page_532,_Bauer_lists_seven_[[allophone]]s_(variant_pronunciations)-128) but its most common pronunciation (its canonical allophone) is the [labiodental fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_labiodental_fricative "Voiceless labiodental fricative"), IPA [f] (as in the English word ***f**ill*). Another allophone is the [voiceless bilabial fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_fricative "Voiceless bilabial fricative"), IPA [ɸ], which is usually supposed to be the sole pre-European pronunciation, although linguists are not sure of the truth of this supposition.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] At least until the 1930s, the bilabial fricative was considered to be the correct pronunciation.[[126]](#cite_note-129) The fact that English ⟨f⟩ gets substituted by ⟨p⟩ and not ⟨wh⟩ in borrowings (for example, English *February* becomes *Pēpuere* instead of *\*Whēpuere*) would strongly hint that the Māori did not perceive English /f/ to be the same sound as their ⟨wh⟩.

Because English stops /p, t, k/ primarily have [aspiration](/wiki/Aspirated_consonant "Aspirated consonant"), speakers of English often hear the Māori nonaspirated stops as English /b, d, ɡ/. However, younger Māori[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] speakers tend to aspirate /p, t, k/ as in English.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] English speakers also tend to hear Māori /r/ as English /l/ in certain positions (cf. [Japanese](/wiki/Perception_of_English_/r/_and_/l/_by_Japanese_speakers "Perception of English /r/ and /l/ by Japanese speakers") r).

/ŋ/ can come at the beginning of a word (like 'sing-along' without the "si"), which may be difficult for English speakers outside of New Zealand to manage. In some western areas of the North Island, ⟨h⟩ is pronounced as a glottal stop [[ʔ](/wiki/Glottal_stop "Glottal stop")] instead of [h], and the [digraph](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") ⟨wh⟩ is pronounced as [ʔw] instead of [f] or [ɸ]. /ɾ/ is typically a [flap](/wiki/Alveolar_flap "Alveolar flap"), and occasionally an [approximant](/wiki/Approximant "Approximant").[[20]](#cite_note-Shields126-21)

In borrowings from English, many consonants are substituted by the nearest available Māori consonant. For example, the English affricates /tʃ/ and /dʒ/, and the fricative /s/ are replaced by /h/, /f/ becomes /p/, and /l/ becomes /ɾ/ (the /l/ is sometimes retained in the southern dialect, as noted below).

### Syllables and phonotactics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Syllables and phonotactics")]

[Syllables](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable") in Māori have one of the following forms: V, VV, CV, CVV. This set of four can be summarised by the notation, (C)V(V), in which the segments in parentheses may or may not be present. A syllable cannot begin with two consonant sounds (the [digraphs](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") <ng> and <wh> represent single consonant sounds), and cannot end in a consonant, although some speakers may occasionally devoice a final vowel. All possible **CV** combinations are grammatical, though *wo*, *who*, *wu*, and *whu* occur only in a few loanwords from English such as *wuru*, "wool" and *whutupōro*, "football".[[127]](#cite_note-130)

As in many other Polynesian languages, e.g., Hawaiian, the rendering of [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") from English includes representing every English consonant of the loanword (using the native consonant inventory; English has 24 consonants to 10 for Māori) and breaking up consonant clusters. For example: "Presbyterian" has been borrowed as *Perehipeteriana*; no consonant position in the loanword has been deleted, but /s/ and /b/ have been replaced with /h/ and /p/, respectively.

Only one acquired couplet is prioritised whenever possible, based on perceived strength of consonants. "Treaty" is [[tiriti]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori"), but *tiriti* also yields a homonym that means *street* with an emphasis on the stronger /t/. *kāpene* from *captain* follows a similar pattern following the stronger /p/.[[128]](#cite_note-131) Medial [nasal consonants](/wiki/Nasal_consonant "Nasal consonant") like /ŋ/ succeeded by a final consonant are also reduced this way:[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow200736-132) the *an* in English *bank* [/bæŋk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") for example is reduced to the approximant /e/ with another /e/ as the supporting vowel which merges to [elongate](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length") as [[peːke]](/wiki/Help:IPA/M%C4%81ori "Help:IPA/Māori") hence *pēke* (or formerly written as ⟨[peeke](/wiki/Te_Peeke_o_Aotearoa "Te Peeke o Aotearoa")⟩).

Stress is typically within the last four vowels of a word, with long vowels and diphthongs counting double. That is, on the last four moras. However, stressed moras are longer than unstressed moras, so the word does not have the precision in Māori that it does in some other languages. It falls preferentially on the first long vowel, on the first diphthong if there is no long vowel (though for some speakers never a final diphthong), and on the first syllable otherwise. Compound words (such as names) may have a stressed syllable in each component word. In long sentences, the final syllable before a pause may have a stress in preference to the normal stressed syllable.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Dialects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/M%C4%81ori_dialects_map.svg/250px-M%C4%81ori_dialects_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:M%C4%81ori_dialects_map.svg)

North Island dialects[[130]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow200742-133)

Biggs proposed that historically there were two major dialect groups, North Island and South Island, and that South Island Māori is extinct.[[131]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs198865-134) Biggs has analysed North Island Māori as comprising a western group and an eastern group with the boundary between them running pretty much along the island's north–south axis.[[132]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997xxvi-135)

Within these broad divisions regional variations occur, and individual regions show tribal variations. The major differences occur in the pronunciation of words, variation of vocabulary, and idiom. A fluent speaker of Māori has no problem understanding other dialects.

There is no significant variation in grammar between dialects. "Most of the tribal variation in grammar is a matter of preferences: speakers of one area might prefer one grammatical form to another, but are likely on occasion to use the non-preferred form, and at least to recognise and understand it."[[133]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1993xxi–xxii-136) Vocabulary and pronunciation vary to a greater extent, but this does not pose barriers to communication.

### Northern dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Northern dialects")]

In the northern dialects, particularly in [Muriwhenua](/wiki/Muriwhenua "Muriwhenua") and parts of [Ngāpuhi](/wiki/Ng%C4%81puhi "Ngāpuhi"), the [digraph](/wiki/Digraph_(orthography) "Digraph (orthography)") *wh* is not pronounced as ⟨f⟩, as it is in most of the other dialects, but as a [voiceless bilabial fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_bilabial_fricative "Voiceless bilabial fricative") ⟨ɸ⟩. Some speakers also reduce this sound to ⟨h⟩, particularly in words beginning with the causative prefix *whaka-* (e.g. *whakarongo*), leading to the pronunciation being heard as *haka-* (e.g. *hakarongo* or '*hakarongo*).[[134]](#cite_note-threat-137)[[135]](#cite_note-138)

Speakers of the northern dialect, like other dialects, also have preferences for specific renderings of words (e.g. *kāhore* instead of *kāore*, *kaurua/kourua* instead of *kōrua*, etc.), or entirely unique words (e.g. *kūkupa* instead of *[kererū](/wiki/Kerer%C5%AB "Kererū")*, *whareiti* instead of *wharepaku*, etc.)[[134]](#cite_note-threat-137)[[136]](#cite_note-139)

### Eastern and western dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Eastern and western dialects")]

In the southwest of the North Island, in the [Whanganui](/wiki/Whanganui "Whanganui") and [Taranaki](/wiki/Taranaki "Taranaki") regions, the phoneme ⟨h⟩ is a [glottal stop](/wiki/Glottal_stop "Glottal stop") and the phoneme ⟨wh⟩ is [ʔw]. This difference was the subject of considerable debate during the 1990s and 2000s over the then-proposed change of the name of the city Wanganui to Whanganui.

In [Tūhoe](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_T%C5%ABhoe "Ngāi Tūhoe") and the Eastern [Bay of Plenty](/wiki/Bay_of_Plenty_Region "Bay of Plenty Region") (northeastern North Island) ⟨ng⟩ has merged with ⟨n⟩.

Speakers in [Waikato–Tainui](/wiki/Waikato_Tainui "Waikato Tainui"), also have preferences for renderings of words (e.g. *ngētehi*, *ngāna* and *ngēnā* instead of *ētahi*, *āna* or *ēnā*. *Pēwhea* and *Kōwhatu* instead of *pēhea* and *pōhatu*.)

### Southern dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Southern dialects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Hotere_annotation.png/250px-Hotere_annotation.png)](/wiki/File:Hotere_annotation.png)

Part of the annotation to a [Ralph Hotere](/wiki/Ralph_Hotere "Ralph Hotere") exhibition at the [Dunedin Public Art Gallery](/wiki/Dunedin_Public_Art_Gallery "Dunedin Public Art Gallery"), written bilingually in English and southern Māori. Note several regional variations, such as *nohoka* (*nohoanga*, a place or seat), *tikaka* (*tikanga*, customs), *āhana/ōhona* (*ana / ōna*, [alienable and inalienable](/wiki/Alienable_and_inalienable_possession "Alienable and inalienable possession") "his"), *pako* (*pango*, black), and *whaka* (*whanga*, harbour).

In South Island dialects, *ng* merged into *k* in many regions. Thus *Kāi Tahu* and *[Ngāi Tahu](/wiki/Ng%C4%81i_Tahu "Ngāi Tahu")* are variations in the name of the same [iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi") (the latter form is the one used in Acts of Parliament). Since 2000, the government has altered the official names of several southern place names to the southern dialect forms by replacing *ng* with *k*. New Zealand's highest mountain, known for centuries as *Aoraki* in southern Māori dialects that merge *ng* with *k*, and as *Aorangi* by other Māori, was later named "Mount Cook". Now its sole official name is *[Aoraki / Mount Cook](/wiki/Aoraki_/_Mount_Cook "Aoraki / Mount Cook")*, which favours the local dialect form. Similarly, the Māori name for [Stewart Island](/wiki/Stewart_Island "Stewart Island"), *Rakiura*, is cognate with the name of the [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_Region "Canterbury Region") town of [Rangiora](/wiki/Rangiora "Rangiora"). Likewise, [Dunedin](/wiki/Dunedin "Dunedin")'s main research library, the [Hocken Collections](/wiki/Hocken_Collections "Hocken Collections"), has the name *Uare Taoka o Hākena* rather than the northern (standard) *Te Whare Taonga o Hākena*.[[d]](#cite_note-140) Maarire Goodall and [George Griffiths](/wiki/George_Griffiths_(historian) "George Griffiths (historian)") say there is also a voicing of *k* to *g*, which explains why the region of [Otago](/wiki/Otago "Otago") (southern dialect) and the settlement it is named after – [Otakou](/wiki/Otakou "Otakou") (standard Māori) – vary in spelling (the pronunciation of the latter having changed over time to accommodate the northern spelling).[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodallGriffiths198046–48-141)

The standard Māori *r* is also found occasionally changed to an *l* in these southern dialects and the *wh* to *w*. These changes are most commonly found in place names, such as [Lake Waihola](/wiki/Lake_Waihola "Lake Waihola"),[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodallGriffiths1980p._50:_"Southern_dialect_for_'wai'_–_water,_'hora'_–_spread_out"-142) and the nearby coastal settlement of [Wangaloa](/wiki/Wangaloa "Wangaloa") (which would, in standard Māori, be rendered *Whangaroa*), and [Little Akaloa](/wiki/Little_Akaloa "Little Akaloa"), on [Banks Peninsula](/wiki/Banks_Peninsula "Banks Peninsula"). Goodall and Griffiths suggest that final vowels are given a centralised pronunciation as [schwa](/wiki/Schwa "Schwa") or that they are [elided](/wiki/Elision "Elision") (pronounced indistinctly or not at all), resulting in such seemingly bastardised place names as [The Kilmog](/wiki/The_Kilmog "The Kilmog"), which in standard Māori would have been rendered *Kirimoko*, but which in southern dialect would have been pronounced very much as the current name suggests.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGoodallGriffiths1980p._45:_"This_hill_[The_Kilmog]...has_a_much_debated_name,_but_its_origins_are_clear_to_Kaitahu_and_the_word_illustrates_several_major_features_of_the_southern_dialect._First_we_must_restore_the_truncated_final_vowel_(in_this_case_to_both_parts_of_the_name,_'kilimogo')._Then_substitute_r_for_l,_k_for_g,_to_obtain_the_northern_pronunciation,_'kirimoko'...._Though_final_vowels_existed_in_Kaitahu_dialect,_the_elision_was_so_nearly_complete_that_[[pākehā]]_recorders_often_omitted_them_entirely"-143) This same elision is found in numerous other southern placenames, such as the two small settlements called The Kaik (from the term for a fishing village, *kāinga* in standard Māori), near [Palmerston](/wiki/Palmerston,_New_Zealand "Palmerston, New Zealand") and [Akaroa](/wiki/Akaroa "Akaroa"), and the early spelling of [Lake Wakatipu](/wiki/Lake_Wakatipu "Lake Wakatipu") as *Wagadib*. In standard Māori, Wakatipu would have been rendered *Whakatipua*, showing further the elision of a final vowel.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Despite the dialect being officially regarded as extinct,[[e]](#cite_note-145) its use in signage and official documentation is encouraged by many government and educational agencies in Otago and [Southland](/wiki/Southland,_New_Zealand "Southland, New Zealand").[[141]](#cite_note-146)[[142]](#cite_note-147)

## Grammar and syntax

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Grammar and syntax")]

Māori has mostly a [verb-subject-object](/wiki/Verb%E2%80%93subject%E2%80%93object_word_order "Verb–subject–object word order") (VSO) [word order](/wiki/Word_order "Word order").[[143]](#cite_note-148) It is also [analytical](/wiki/Isolating_language "Isolating language"), featuring almost no [inflection](/wiki/Inflection "Inflection"), and makes extensive use of grammatical particles to indicate grammatical categories of tense, mood, aspect, case, topicalisation, among others. The personal pronouns have a distinction in [clusivity](/wiki/Clusivity "Clusivity"), singular, dual and plural numbers,[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199832–33-149) and the genitive pronouns have different classes (*a* class, *o* class and neutral) according to whether the possession is [alienable](/wiki/Alienable_and_inalienable_possession "Alienable and inalienable possession") or the possessor has control of the relationship (*a* category), or the possession is [inalienable](/wiki/Inalienable_possession "Inalienable possession") or the possessor has no control over the relationship (*o* category), and a third [neutral class](/wiki/Possessive_determiner "Possessive determiner") that only occurs for singular pronouns and must be followed by a noun.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199846–48-150) There is also [subject-object-verb](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93object%E2%80%93verb_word_order "Subject–object–verb word order") (SOV) word order used in agent emphatic sentences.[[146]](#cite_note-151) Examples of this include *Nāku te ngohi i tunu* ("I cooked the fish"; literally *I the fish cooked*) and *Mā wai te haka e kaea?* ("Who will lead the haka?"). Agent emphatic sentences can also take the [subject-verb-object](/wiki/Subject%E2%80%93verb%E2%80%93object_word_order "Subject–verb–object word order") (SVO) word order.[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199887-152) In this order the example sentences would be rendered as *Nāku i tunu te ngohi* and *Mā wai e kaea te haka?*

### Bases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Bases")]

Biggs (1998) developed an analysis that the basic unit of Māori speech is the phrase rather than the word.[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs19983-153) The lexical word forms the "base" of the phrase. Biggs identifies five types of bases.

**Noun** bases include those bases that can take a definite article, but cannot occur as the nucleus of a verbal phrase; for example: *ika* (fish) or *rākau* (tree).[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199854–55-154) Plurality is marked by various means, including the definite article (singular *te*, plural *ngā*),[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997144–147-155) deictic particles *tērā rākau* (that tree), *ērā rākau* (those trees),[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997153–154-156) possessives *taku whare* (my house), *aku whare* (my houses).[[152]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997394–396-157) A few nouns lengthen a vowel in the plural, such as *wahine* (woman); *wāhine* (women).[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997160-158) In general, bases used as qualifiers follow the base they qualify, e.g. "matua wahine" (mother, female elder) from "matua" (parent, elder) "wahine" (woman).[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs1998153-159)

**Universal** bases are verbs which can be used passively. When used passively, these verbs take a passive form. Biggs gives three examples of universals in their passive form: *inumia* (drunk), *tangihia* (wept for), and *kīa* (said).[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199855-160)

**Stative** bases serve as bases usable as verbs but not available for passive use, such as *ora*, alive or *tika*, correct.[[155]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199855-160) Grammars generally refer to them as "stative verbs". When used in sentences, statives require different syntax than other verb-like bases.[[156]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199823–24-161)

**Locative** bases can follow the locative particle *ki* (to, towards) directly, such as *runga*, above, *waho*, outside, and placenames (*ki Tamaki*, to Auckland).[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199857-162)

**Personal** bases take the personal article *a* after *ki*, such as names of people (*ki a Hohepa*, to Joseph), personified houses, personal pronouns, *wai?* who? and *mea*, so-and-so.[[157]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199857-162)

### Particles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Particles")]

Like all other Polynesian languages, Māori has a rich array of particles, which include verbal particles, pronouns, locative particles, articles and possessives.

Verbal particles indicate aspectual, tense-related or modal properties of the verb which they relate to. They include:

* *i* (past)
* *e* (non-past)
* *i te* (past continuous)
* *kei te* (present continuous)[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs1998107–108-163)
* *kua* (perfect)
* *e ... ana* (imperfect, continuous)
* *ka* (inceptive, future)
* *kia* (desiderative)
* *me* (prescriptive)
* *kei* (warning, "lest")
* *ina* or *ana* (punctative-conditional, "if and when")[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer199784–100-164)
* *kāti* (cessative)[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997447-165)
* *ai* (habitual)[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer199798-166)

Locative particles (prepositions) refer to position in time and/or space, and include:

* *ki* (to, towards)
* *kei* (at)
* *i* (past position)
* *hei* (future position)[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer199730-167)

Possessives fall into one of two classes of prepositions marked by *a* and *o*, depending on the dominant versus subordinate relationship between possessor and possessed: *ngā tamariki a te matua*, the children of the parent but *te matua o ngā tamariki*, the parent of the children.[[163]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199842-168)

### Determiners

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Determiners")]

#### Articles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Articles")]

| Definite | *te* | *ngā* |

The definite articles are *te* (singular) and *ngā* (plural).[[164]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs19987–8-169)[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow2007141-170) Several other determiners termed definitives are related to the singular definite article *te*, such as the definitive [possessive constructions](#Possessive_pronouns) with *tā* and *tō* and the [demonstrative determiners](#Demonstrative_determiners_and_adverbs).[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199846–48-150)

The Māori definite articles are frequently used where the equivalent, *the*, is not used in English, such as when referring generically to an entire class. In these cases, the singular *te* can even be used with a morphologically plural noun, as in

te

"children (in general)"

as opposed to

"the (specific group of) children"

[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow2007141-170)

In other syntactic environments, the definite article may be used to introduce a noun-phrase which is pragmatically indefinite due to the restrictions on the use of *he* as discussed below.[[165]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow2007141-170)

The indefinite article *he* is used most frequently in the predicate and occasionally in the subject of the sentence, although it is not allowed in subject position in all sentence types.[[166]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow2007143,_144-171) In the predicate, the indefinite article *he* can introduce either nouns or adjectives.[[167]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow2007143-172) The article either can be translated to the English 'a' or 'some', but the number will not be indicated by *he*. With nouns that show morphological number, *he* may be used either with singular or plural forms. The indefinite article *he* when used with mass nouns like water and sand will always mean 'some'.[[168]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs19987-173)

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *he tāne* | a man | some men |
| *he kōtiro* | a girl | some girls |
| *he kāinga* | a village | some villages |
| *he āporo* | an apple | some apples |
| *he tangata* | a person | – |
| *he tāngata* | – | some people |

The indefinite article *he* is highly restricted in its use and is incompatible with a preceding preposition. For this reason, it cannot be used in the grammatical object of the sentence as these are marked prepositionally, either with *i* or *ki*. In many cases, speakers simply use the definite articles *te* and *ngā* in positions where *he* is disallowed, however the indefinite articles *tētahi* and *ētahi* may be used in these situations to emphasise the indefiniteness.[[169]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow2007144-174)

#### Demonstrative determiners and adverbs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Demonstrative determiners and adverbs")]

Demonstratives occur after the noun and have a deictic function, and include *tēnei*, this (near me), *tēnā*, that (near you), *tērā*, that (far from us both), and *taua*, the aforementioned (anaphoric). These demonstratives, having a connection to the definite article *te* are termed definitives. Other definitives include *tēhea?* (which?), and *tētahi*, (a certain). The plural is formed just by dropping the *t*: *tēnei* (this), *ēnei* (these). The related adverbs are *nei* (here), *nā* (there, near you), *rā* (over there, near him).[[172]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997152–154-177)

Phrases introduced by demonstratives can also be expressed using the definite article *te* or *ngā* preceding a noun followed by one of the deictic particles *nei*, *nā* or *rā*. The *t* of the singular definite article appears in the singular demonstratives but is replaced by *∅* in the plural, having no connection with *ngā* in the majority of dialects.

### Pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Pronouns")]

#### Personal pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Personal pronouns")]

Pronouns have singular, dual and plural number. Different first-person forms in both the dual and the plural are used for groups inclusive or exclusive of the person(s) addressed.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Pronouns_In_New_Zealand_Te_Reo_M%C4%81ori.svg/500px-Pronouns_In_New_Zealand_Te_Reo_M%C4%81ori.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Pronouns_In_New_Zealand_Te_Reo_M%C4%81ori.svg)

* 1
* 2
* 3

|  | | Singular | Dual | Plural |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1st person | exclusive | *[au](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/au#Māori "wikt:au")* / *[ahau](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/ahau#Māori "wikt:ahau")* | *[māua](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/m%C4%81ua#Māori "wikt:māua")* | *[mātou](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/m%C4%81tou#Māori "wikt:mātou")* |
| inclusive | *[tāua](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/t%C4%81ua#Māori "wikt:tāua")* | *[tātou](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/t%C4%81tou#Māori "wikt:tātou")* |
| 2nd person | | *[koe](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/koe#Māori "wikt:koe")* | *[kōrua](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/k%C5%8Drua#Māori "wikt:kōrua")* | *[koutou](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/koutou#Māori "wikt:koutou")* |
