Like other Polynesian languages, Māori has three [numbers](/wiki/Grammatical_number "Grammatical number") for pronouns and possessives: singular, [dual](/wiki/Dual_(grammatical_number) "Dual (grammatical number)") and plural. For example: *ia* (he/she), *rāua* (those two), *rātou* (they, three or more). Māori pronouns and possessives further distinguish exclusive "we" from inclusive "we", second and third. It has the plural pronouns: *mātou* (we, exc), *tātou* (we, inc), *koutou* (you), *rātou* (they). The language features the dual pronouns: *māua* (me and another), *tāua* (me and you), *kōrua* (you two), *rāua* (those two). The difference between [exclusive and inclusive](/wiki/Clusivity "Clusivity") lies in the treatment of the person addressed. *Mātou* refers to the speaker and others but not the person or persons spoken to ("I and some others but not you"), and *tātou* refers to the speaker, the person or persons spoken to and everyone else ("you, I and others"):[[175]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997261–262-180)

* *Tēnā koe*: hello (to one person)
* *Tēnā kōrua*: hello (to two people)
* *Tēnā koutou*: hello (to more than two people)[[176]](#cite_note-181)

#### Possessive pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Possessive pronouns")]

The possessive pronouns vary according to person, number, [clusivity](/wiki/Clusivity "Clusivity"), and possessive class (a class or o class). Example: *tāku pene* (my pen), *āku pene* (my pens). For dual and plural subject pronouns, the possessive form is analytical, by just putting the possessive particle (**tā/tō** for singular objects or **ā/ō** for plural objects) before the personal pronouns, e.g. *tā tātou karaihe* (our class), *tō rāua whare* (their [dual] house); *ā tātou karaihe* (our classes). The neuter one must be followed by a noun and only occur for singular first, second and third persons. *Taku* is my, *aku* is my (plural, for many possessed items). The plural is made by deleting the initial [t].[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199846–48-150)

| Subject | | Object | | | | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Number | Person | Singular | | | Plural | | |
| a class | o class | neutral | a class | o class | neutral |
| Singular | 1 | tāku | tōku | taku | āku | ōku | aku |
| 2 | tāu | tōu | tō | āu | ōu | ō |
| 3 | tāna | tōna | tana | āna | ōna | ana |

* *hea*/*whea* ('where')[[177]](#cite_note-182)
* *nō hea* ('whence')

### Phrase grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Phrase grammar")]

A phrase spoken in Māori can be broken up into two parts: the "nucleus" or "head" and "periphery" (modifiers, determiners). The nucleus can be thought of as the meaning and is the centre of the phrase, whereas the periphery is where the grammatical meaning is conveyed and occurs before and/or after the nucleus.[[179]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs19984-184)

| Periphery | Nucleus | Periphery |
| --- | --- | --- |
| *te* | *whare* | *nei* |
| *ki te* | *whare* |  |

The nucleus *whare* can be translated as "house", the periphery *te* is similar to an article "the" and the periphery *nei* indicates proximity to the speaker. The whole phrase, *te whare nei*, can then be translated as "this house".[[180]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs19985-185)

#### Phrasal particles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Phrasal particles")]

A definite and declarative sentence (may be a copulative sentence) begins with the declarative particle *ko*.[[181]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199815–17-186) If the sentence is topicalised (agent topic, only in non-present sentences) the sentence begins with the particle ***nā*** (past tense) or the particle ***mā*** (future, imperfective) followed by the agent/subject. In these cases the word order changes to subject-verb-object or subject-object-verb. These are the agent emphatic sentences discussed earlier. The agent topicalising particles can contract with singular personal pronouns and vary according to the possessive classes: *nāku* can be thought of as meaning "as for me" and behave like an emphatic or dative pronoun.[[182]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBiggs199887–89-187)

Agent topic pronouns

|  | Past | Future |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1S | nāku/nōku | māku/mōku |
| 2S | nāu/nōu | māu/mō |
| 3S | nāna/nōna | māna/mōna |

#### Case particles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Case particles")]

* Nominative: ko[[183]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997181-188)
* Accusative: i[[184]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997175–176-189)
* Dative/directional locative: ki[[185]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997176–179-190)
* Genitive: a/o[[186]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997183–184-191)

### Negation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Negation")]

Forming negative phrases in Māori is quite grammatically complex. There are several different negators which are used under various specific circumstances.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauerEvansParker2001139-192) The main negators are as follows:[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauerEvansParker2001139-192)

| Negator | Description |
| --- | --- |
| *kāo* | Negative answer to a polar question. |
| *kāore/kāhore/kāre* | The most common verbal negator. |
| *kore* | A strong negator, equivalent to 'never'. |
| *kaua e* | Negative imperatives; prohibitive |
| *ehara* | Negation for copulative phrases, topicalised and equative phrases |

*Kīhai* and *tē* are two negators which may be seen in specific dialects or older texts, but are not widely used.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauerEvansParker2001139-192) The most common negator is *kāhore*, which may occur in one of four forms, with the *kāo* form only being used in response to a question.[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauerEvansParker2001139-192) Negative phrases, besides using *kāore*, also affect the form of verbal particles, as illustrated below.

Verbal particles[[187]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauerEvansParker2001139-192)

|  | Positive | Negative |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Past | i | i |
| Future | ka | i/e |
| Present | kei te | i te |
| Imperfect | e...ana | |
| Past perfect | kua | kia |

The general usage of *kāhore* can be seen in the following examples. The subject is usually raised in negative phrases, although this is not obligatory.[[188]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauerEvansParker2001141-193) Each example of a negative phrase is presented with its analogue positive phrase for comparison.

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| E  T/A  haere  move  ana  T/A  tātou  1PL.INCL  āpōpō  tomorrow  E haere ana tātou āpōpō  T/A move T/A 1PL.INCL tomorrow  'We are going tomorrow'[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauerEvansParker2001140-194)  Kua  T/A  tae  arrive  mai  hither  he  a  tāngata  people  Kua tae mai he tāngata  T/A arrive hither a people  'Some people have arrived'[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauerEvansParker2001140-194) | **Kāhore**  **NEG**  tātou  1PL.INCL  e  T/A  haere  move  ana  T/A  āpōpō  tomorrow  **Kāhore** tātou e haere ana āpōpō  **NEG** 1PL.INCL T/A move T/A tomorrow  'We are not going tomorrow'[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauerEvansParker2001140-194)  **Kāhore**  **NEG**  anō  yet  he  a  tāngata  people  kia  SUBJ  tae  arrive  mai  hither  **Kāhore** anō he tāngata kia tae mai  **NEG** yet a people SUBJ arrive hither  'Nobody has arrived yet'[[189]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauerEvansParker2001140-194) |

### Passive sentences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Passive sentences")]

The passive voice of verbs is made by a suffix to the verb. For example, -ia (or just -a if the verb ends in [i]). The other passive suffixes, some of which are very rare, are: -hanga/-hia/-hina/-ina/-kia/-kina/-mia/-na/-nga/-ngia/-ria/-rina/-tia/-whia/-whina/.[[190]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow2015113-195) The use of the passive suffix -ia is given in this sentence: *Kua hanga**ia** te marae e ngā tohunga* (The marae has been built by the experts). The active form of this sentence is rendered as: *Kua hanga ngā tohunga i te marae* (The experts have built the marae). It can be seen that the active sentence contains the object marker 'i', that is not present in the passive sentence, while the passive sentence has the agent marker 'e', which is not present in the active sentence.[[191]](#cite_note-196)

### Polar questions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Polar questions")]

Polar questions (yes/no questions) can be made by changing the intonation of the sentence. The answers may be *āe* (yes) or *kāo* (no).[[192]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997424–427-197)

### Derivational morphology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Derivational morphology")]

Although Māori is mostly analytical there are several derivational affixes:

* -anga, -hanga, -ranga, -tanga (-ness, -ity) (the suffix depends on whether the verb takes, respectively, the -ia, -hia, -ria or -tia passive suffixes) (e.g. *pōti* 'vote', *pōtitanga* 'election')
* -nga (nominaliser)[[193]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997517–524-198)
* kai- (agentive noun)[[194]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer199725–26-199) (e.g. *mahi* 'work', *kaimahi* 'worker/employee')
* ma- (adjectives)[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHarlow2015112-200)
* tua- (ordinal numerals)[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer1997282–283-201) (e.g. *tahi* 'one', *tuatahi* 'first/primary')
* whaka- (causative prefix)[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBauer199744–45-202)

## Influence on New Zealand English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Influence on New Zealand English")]

Main article: [Māori language influence on New Zealand English](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_language_influence_on_New_Zealand_English "Māori language influence on New Zealand English")

[New Zealand English](/wiki/New_Zealand_English "New Zealand English") (NZE) has gained many loanwords from Māori;[[198]](#cite_note-203)[[199]](#cite_note-Andersen1946-204)[[200]](#cite_note-205)[[201]](#cite_note-206) those most commonly used are proper nouns including place names, and words that refer to New Zealand plants and animals which frequently have no other English name.[[202]](#cite_note-deBres-207) For example, the [kiwi](/wiki/Kiwi_(bird) "Kiwi (bird)"), the national bird, takes its name from *te reo*.[[203]](#cite_note-208) Other words and phrases for which English alternatives do exist are also in common use; however, they are more likely to be used if they carry specific meanings with respect to Māori culture, such as *[kaumātua](/wiki/Kaum%C4%81tua "Kaumātua")* ("elder"), *[iwi](/wiki/Iwi "Iwi")* ("tribe, nation"), and *[karakia](/wiki/Karakia "Karakia")* ("prayer").[[204]](#cite_note-CaludeMillerPagel-209)

*[Kia ora](/wiki/Kia_ora "Kia ora")* (literally "be healthy") is a widely adopted greeting of Māori origin, with the intended meaning of "hello".[[205]](#cite_note-210) It can also mean "thank you", or signify agreement with a speaker at a meeting. The Māori greetings *tēnā koe* (to one person), *tēnā kōrua* (to two people) or *tēnā koutou* (to three or more people) are also widely used, as are farewells such as *haere rā*. The Māori phrase *kia kaha* ("be strong") is frequently encountered as an indication of moral support for someone starting a stressful undertaking or otherwise in a difficult situation. Many other words such as *[whānau](/wiki/Wh%C4%81nau "Whānau")* ("family") and *kai* ("food") are also widely understood and used by New Zealanders. The Māori phrase *Ka kite anō* ("until I see you again") is quite commonly used.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Māori speakers of NZE use Māori loanwords more often than non-Māori speakers, and Māori women more often than Māori men; no gender effect is observed among non-Māori NZE speakers.[[204]](#cite_note-CaludeMillerPagel-209) Māori NZE speakers use Māori loanwords more often when their audience is exclusively Māori than when they have non-Māori listeners.[[204]](#cite_note-CaludeMillerPagel-209)

Often the choice to use these words reflects an expression of social or political identity on the part of the speaker – as may also a choice not to use a Māori word when one exists.[[202]](#cite_note-deBres-207) Although the use of Māori words in English correlates to some degree with the speaker's support for *te reo*, some non-Māori supporters choose not to use Māori words out of concern that such words do not "belong" to them.[[202]](#cite_note-deBres-207) Younger NZE speakers are more likely than older speakers to use Māori words denoting non-material aspects of culture, such as *[tapu](/wiki/Tapu_(Polynesian_culture) "Tapu (Polynesian culture)")* ("sacred or cursed"), *kōrero* ("speech"), or *kaitiakitanga* ("stewardship").[[202]](#cite_note-deBres-207)

In 2023, 47 words or expressions from New Zealand English, mostly of Māori etymology, were added to the Oxford English Dictionary.[[206]](#cite_note-211)

### Pronunciation of Māori words in New Zealand English

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Pronunciation of Māori words in New Zealand English")]

The pronunciation of Māori loanwords (especially names) when speaking English, specifically the degree to which the words are assimilated to NZE phonology, is widely perceived in New Zealand as a social marker of the speaker's attitudes to the Māori language and people. One magazine columnist is quoted as saying

:   How a Pakeha chooses to pronounce "Māori" determines precisely where they fit on the [PC](/wiki/Political_correctness "Political correctness") scale. There are 11 possible variations, from "may-o-ree" at one end to [Kim Hill](/wiki/Kim_Hill_(broadcaster) "Kim Hill (broadcaster)")'s "mow-rri" at the other. The key is how broad you make your "a" and whether you roll your "r". Such small things, but they can make the difference between being taken for a Neanderthal bozo and getting on a polytech payroll.[[202]](#cite_note-deBres-207)

In a 2018 interview, Māori actor-director [Taika Waititi](/wiki/Taika_Waititi "Taika Waititi") described New Zealand as "racist as fuck" primarily on the basis that "people just flat-out refuse to pronounce Māori names correctly". His comments aroused considerable backlash in the New Zealand media.[[207]](#cite_note-racistAF-212)

Among Māori people, the use of (non-assimilated) Māori pronunciations reflects the individual's degree of integration into the Māori community.[[202]](#cite_note-deBres-207) Among non-Māori, supporters of *te reo* view Māori pronunciations as a marker of that support, but frequently do not use them out of concerns about getting them wrong or not being understood.[[202]](#cite_note-deBres-207) Public service broadcaster [Radio New Zealand](/wiki/Radio_New_Zealand "Radio New Zealand")'s policy is to pronounce Māori words in English as they would be pronounced in Māori.[[208]](#cite_note-rnzPronunciation-213)

Māori is available on [Google Translate](/wiki/Google_Translate "Google Translate"), [Microsoft Translator](/wiki/Microsoft_Translator "Microsoft Translator"), [Yandex Translate](/wiki/Yandex_Translate "Yandex Translate"), and [DeepL Translator](/wiki/DeepL_Translator "DeepL Translator").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Sample text

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Sample text")]

Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") in [Māori](/wiki/M%C4%81ori_Language_Day "Māori Language Day"):

> *I te whānautanga mai o te tangata, kāhore ōna here, e ōrite ana tōna mana me ōna tika ki te katoa. Ka whakatōkia ki roto i te tangata he wairua, he hinengaro hoki, ā, me mahi tahi ia ki ngā tāngata o te ao i runga i te āhua o te tuakana me te teina.*[[216]](#cite_note-221)

Article 1 of the [Universal Declaration of Human Rights](/wiki/Universal_Declaration_of_Human_Rights "Universal Declaration of Human Rights") in English:

> All human beings are born free and equal in dignity and rights. They are endowed with reason and conscience and should act towards one another in a spirit of brotherhood.

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=M%C4%81ori_language&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-6)** *[Tā moko](/wiki/T%C4%81_moko "Tā moko")* facial tattoos, as used in the 19th century, have been posited as a form of textual language, but do not correlate to spoken Māori.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
2. **[^](#cite_ref-120)** Bauer mentions that Biggs 1961 announced a similar finding.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-122)** Bauer even raised the possibility of analysing Māori as really having six vowel phonemes, *a, ā, e, i, o, u* ([a, aː, ɛ, i, ɔ, ʉ]).
4. **[^](#cite_ref-140)** The Hocken Library contains several early journals and notebooks of early missionaries documenting the vagaries of the southern dialect. Several of them are shown at Blackman, A. [*Some Sources for Southern Maori dialect*](https://blogs.otago.ac.nz/thehockenblog/2011/07/07/some-sources-for-southern-maori-dialect/).
5. **[^](#cite_ref-145)** As with many "dead" languages, there is a possibility that the southern dialect may be revived, especially with the encouragement mentioned. "The [Murihiku](/wiki/Murihiku "Murihiku") language – Mulihig' being probably better expressive of its state in 1844 – lives on in Watkin's vocabulary list and in many [muttonbirding](/wiki/Sooty_shearwater "Sooty shearwater") terms still in use, and may flourish again in the new climate of [Maoritaka](/wiki/Maoritanga "Maoritanga")."[[140]](#cite_note-144)
